remediated_from_v1: F002-v1: Audit logs absent — FIXED (all mutations logged)
F006-v1: CSP/HSTS missing in Caddy — FIXED
F010-v1: No security headers in next.config.ts — FIXED

findings.title: IDOR on cashier session access — any authenticated user can read any session
findings.what: GET /api/v1/sales/sessions/{session_id} fetches any cashier session without verifying the requesting user owns it
findings.why: Any cashier (or any other role) can read financial reconciliation data (starting cash, closing amounts, discrepancy) from another cashier's session by guessing or iterating UUIDs. UUID brute-force is impractical, but authenticated insiders can access sensitive data of colleagues.
findings.fix: In get_session_by_id: load the session, then check `if cashier_session.cashier_id != current_user.id and current_user.role not in ('admin', 'supervisor'): raise HTTPException(403)`. Add equivalent check in the service layer.

findings.title: IDOR on individual sale access — any authenticated user can read any sale
findings.what: GET /api/v1/sales/{sale_id} returns full sale details (customer, items, payment method, amounts) without ownership or role check
findings.why: A cashier can read sales created by other cashiers, accessing customer data, payment references, and business intelligence. An attacker who compromises a low-privilege account can enumerate all sales.
findings.fix: Add ownership check: `if sale.cashier_id != current_user.id and current_user.role not in ('admin', 'supervisor'): raise HTTPException(403)`. Apply same pattern to void_sale and any other sale-by-ID endpoints.

findings.title: Weak supervisor authorization on void_sale — no location/cashier scope enforcement
findings.what: POST /api/v1/sales/{sale_id}/void only checks role=supervisor; does not verify the supervisor manages the cashier who created the sale
findings.why: A supervisor at one location can void sales created by cashiers at any other location, enabling unauthorized financial manipulation across the business.
findings.fix: In void_sale service: check that `sale.cashier_id == current_user.id OR current_user.role == 'admin'`. For multi-location deployments, additionally scope by location_id or supervisor assignment.

findings.title: Default admin password 'Admin123!' — startup warning but no enforcement
findings.what: ADMIN_INITIAL_PASSWORD defaults to 'Admin123!' in config.py. A warning is logged at startup but nothing blocks the app from running with this default.
findings.why: If ADMIN_INITIAL_PASSWORD is not overridden in the .env file (e.g., fresh install, copied config), the admin account has a publicly known password attackable by credential stuffing.
findings.fix: Enforce non-default password at startup: if settings.admin_initial_password == DEFAULT, raise RuntimeError in production (ENV=production). Alternatively, generate a random password and display it once on first boot, then require reset.

findings.title: Tenant isolation absent at DB query level
findings.what: All service queries do not filter by tenant_id. Models have tenant_id with a hardcoded default UUID but queries never enforce tenant boundaries.
findings.why: If multi-tenant deployment is ever attempted, all data from all tenants is accessible to any authenticated user. Also creates IDOR risk if tenant isolation is added inconsistently later.
findings.fix: Add tenant_id to all WHERE clauses as a baseline. Create a TenantContext dependency that injects tenant_id from the authenticated user. Fail closed: 404 if entity.tenant_id != current_user.tenant_id.

findings.title: backend/.env.test committed to git — test credentials in version control
findings.what: backend/.env.test is tracked by git and contains DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY (test values). Added to .gitignore but still tracked.
findings.why: Although credentials are test-only (not production), this normalizes credential storage in git. Developers may base production configs on this pattern. SECRET_KEY format is exposed.
findings.fix: Run `git rm --cached backend/.env.test` to untrack. Provide backend/.env.test.example with placeholder values. Already added to .gitignore in a prior fix.

findings.title: Missing rate limit on gift card redemption endpoint
findings.what: POST /api/v1/gift-cards/{code}/redeem has no @limiter.limit decorator, allowing unrestricted gift card code enumeration.
findings.why: Attackers can rapidly test gift card codes to find valid balances and drain them, especially if code format is predictable or sequential.
findings.fix: Add `@limiter.limit('5/minute')` decorator to the redeem endpoint. Consider also limiting the lookup endpoint.

findings.title: HSTS header configured on HTTP-only Caddy — header is ineffective
findings.what: Caddyfile sets Strict-Transport-Security header but the server only listens on :80 (HTTP). HSTS is ignored by browsers on non-HTTPS responses.
findings.why: The HSTS header provides no protection. If the deployment is ever HTTPS-enabled and HSTS was relied upon for security, it will have never been processed by browsers.
findings.fix: Either: (a) configure TLS in the Caddyfile to serve on :443 (Caddy auto-HTTPS handles this with a domain name), or (b) remove the HSTS header from the HTTP block and add it only when HTTPS is configured.

findings.title: Login endpoint distinguishes wrong password vs inactive account
findings.what: auth.py returns HTTP 401 for bad credentials vs HTTP 403 for inactive account, allowing username enumeration of known-valid-but-inactive users.
findings.why: Minor information disclosure: an attacker can determine that a username exists and has been deactivated (vs never existed). Low practical impact in a POS context.
findings.fix: Return generic HTTP 401 with identical message for both cases. Move the 'account inactive' distinction to a post-authentication step. Acceptable as-is given POS threat model.

findings.title: CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src
findings.what: Both next.config.ts and caddy/Caddyfile include script-src 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' in the Content-Security-Policy.
findings.why: These weaknesses are required by Next.js for inline scripts and hot module replacement. They reduce XSS mitigation effectiveness. Accepted risk for this stack.
findings.fix: Consider using CSP nonces (Next.js nonce support available in v13+) to eliminate 'unsafe-inline'. 'unsafe-eval' can be removed in production builds. Low priority given Next.js constraints.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication for admin or supervisor accounts
findings.what: Authentication is username+password only with no second factor for privileged roles.
findings.why: If admin credentials are compromised, an attacker gains full system access with no second barrier. Admin can void sales, modify settings, and view all reports.
findings.fix: Add optional TOTP (pyotp) as a second factor, required for admin/supervisor roles. Phased rollout acceptable.
